visits.text: Two rivers converge, whitewashed walls reflect in the water: Bauska Castle occupies a strategic position at the junction of the Mūsa and Mēmele since the 15th century. The Knights of the Livonian Order first built a fortress here, then the Dukes of Courland added a palace with distinctive red tiles. The site mixes medieval ruins and partially restored interiors.

Discover Bauska Castle, where the Knights of the Livonian Order first fortified the small islands at the confluence of two rivers. Later, the Dukes of Courland and Russian tsars raised a new fortification with its distinctive whitewashed walls and red-tiled roofs. The castle's position at the start of the Lielupe River made it strategically vital for centuries of Baltic power struggles.

Marvel at Rundāle Palace, the 'Versailles of Latvia' and one of the most magnificent Baroque palaces in the Baltic region. This 18th-century masterpiece was designed by Bartolomeo Rastrelli, celebrated architect of the Russian imperial court, as a gift from Empress Catherine the Great. The palace's opulent interiors, with their flamboyant Italian-esque extravagance, and the surrounding formal rose gardens and English-style lawns attract thousands of visitors annually.

activities.text: Explore the charming hinterland character of Bauska town, where river channels cut through the heart bordered by family gardens and farmland, spanned by fragile suspension bridges. The pretty market square buzzes with local life, offering authentic insight into small-town Latvian existence. This tranquil setting provides perfect contrast to nearby Rundāle Palace's grandeur.

Explore the charming backwater character of Bauska town, where the river channels flow through the heart lined by allotments and farmlands, crossed by tenuous suspension bridges. The pretty market square bustles with local life, offering an authentic glimpse into small-town Latvian existence. This tranquil setting provides a perfect contrast to the grandeur of nearby Rundāle Palace.

days_plan.itin_title: The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History

visits.text: In 1577, 300 besieged defenders blew themselves up with gunpowder rather than surrender to Ivan the Terrible: Cēsis Medieval Castle has a history to match its imposing ruins. Built from the early 13th century, it was for two centuries the residence of the Grand Master of the Livonian Order. Today you visit the towers by candlelight provided at the entrance—a theatrically effective touch.

The cobbled streets of Cēsis and its white-and-yellow painted wooden houses form one of Latvia's best-preserved historic centers. St. John's Church contains the tombs of Knights of the Livonian Order. Rozu Square and May Park below create a tranquil setting to finish the journey before returning to Riga.

Wander through Cesis, whose medieval glory has been preserved in one of Latvia's best historic town centers. Wide cobblestone streets wind past pretty painted timber facades done in the classic Baltic style, while stuccoed churches and worn courtyards evoke the bustling Middle Age markets that once thrived here. St. John's Church contains the tombs of Livonian Order knights who shaped this region's tumultuous history.

Explore Cesis Castle, a formidable medley of palisades and gatehouses shrouded by forests near the town center. This 13th-century stronghold witnessed 800 years of invasions by Swedish, Russian, Slavic, and Polish forces, and its extensive ruins tell the story of the region's strategic importance. Visitors can climb towers, explore underground passages, and discover tales of medieval Baltic warfare.

Visit the Araisi Archaeological Park, the most popular open-air museum in Latvia, located just a short distance from Cesis. This fascinating site chronicles the lifestyles of early Middle Age Baltic tribes who made their homes in reconstructed timber dwellings on the lake banks. Ruins of a Livonian Order castle and extensive archaeological finds showcase everything from 14th-century fashion to medieval farming innovations.

activities.text: Discover the reconstructed lake site at Āraiši, a few kilometers from Cēsis: wooden dwellings on stilts by a lake recreate the life of the early Baltic tribes of the Middle Ages. It's one of the most original open-air museums in the region, adjacent to the ruins of a Livonian Order castle. Allow 45 minutes for the visit, open May to October.

Experience medieval history firsthand at Araisi's reconstructed lake dwelling settlement, where timber lean-tos and wooden cottages recreate life as it was lived over a thousand years ago. The peaceful lakeside setting adds to the atmosphere of stepping back in time. This unique window into Latvia's deeper past brings the region's pre-Christian heritage vividly to life.

days_plan.itin_title: The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History

visits.text: The 'Latvian Riviera' has earned its credentials: Jūrmala offers 33 km of fine sandy beach bordered by dunes and pine forests. Brezhnev and Khrushchev came here to take the waters. The town owes its reputation to its mineral springs and therapeutic muds that have attracted spa-goers since the 19th century. Main beach at Majori, reachable in 25 minutes by train from Riga.

Walk up Jūras Street to see Art Nouveau and romantic wooden villas from the 1880s-1920s parade by: hundreds of listed or registered wooden houses form a unique open-air museum in the Baltics. Carved verandas, turrets, pastel colors—each building is a variation on the same theme of seaside elegance. Most beautiful on weekdays, when cars have disappeared.

Relax on the golden beaches of Jurmala, the beloved 'Latvian Riviera' stretching nearly 30 kilometers along the curved coastline of the Gulf of Riga. This elegant resort destination has attracted visitors including Soviet leaders Brezhnev and Khrushchev, drawn by its unique microclimate, salty sea air, and renowned mineral mud pools offering holistic health treatments. The Blue Flag beaches of Majori and Bulduri rank among the finest on the Baltic coast.

Experience the therapeutic atmosphere of Jurmala's famous spa traditions, which have been drawing wellness seekers for over a century. The resort towns along this stretch of coast specialize in mineral treatments and health therapies utilizing the region's unique natural resources. Charming wooden Art Nouveau villas line the streets, creating a distinctive architectural character unlike anywhere else in the Baltics.

activities.text: Venture into Ķemeri National Park, a wetland paradise celebrated for its vast peat bogs, pristine marshes, and remarkable biodiversity. Well-maintained boardwalk trails wind through this unique ecosystem, allowing visitors to explore without disturbing the fragile environment. The park offers exceptional birdwatching opportunities with numerous rare species inhabiting these atmospheric wetlands.

Venture into Ķemeri National Park, a protected wetland wonderland famous for its vast peat bogs, pristine marshes, and remarkable biodiversity. Well-maintained boardwalk trails wind through this unique ecosystem, allowing visitors to explore without disturbing the fragile environment. The park offers exceptional birdwatching opportunities with numerous rare species making their home in these atmospheric wetlands.

days_plan.itin_title: The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History

visits.text: At 249 meters wide, the Venta Rapid is Europe's widest waterfall—but only 1.8 meters high. That's Kuldīga's paradox: a cascade that impresses by breadth, not power. In spring, salmon leap upstream; in the 17th century, they were caught in baskets suspended above the current. Free entry, accessible year-round.

Kuldīga looks like a postcard—and it's true. Its leaning wooden cottages from the 1700s, cobbled lanes, and elegant baroque St. Catherine's Church form one of Latvia's best-preserved historic centers. The town prospered under the Duchy of Courland and had no major industry to disfigure it. Avoid July weekends: buses arrive in groups.

Wander through Kuldiga's enchanting Old Town, where crooked timber cottages and cobblestone lanes preserve centuries of Old World charm in the heart of the Kurzeme region. Houses dating from the 1700s create one of Latvia's most attractive historic streetscapes, while arched brick bridges and the elegant Baroque St. Catherine's Church recall the town's prosperous era under the Courland duchy. This chocolate-box town represents authentic Latvian heritage at its finest.

Marvel at the Ventas Rumba, the widest waterfall in Europe stretching 249 meters across the Venta River on the outskirts of town. These roaring cascades create a spectacular natural phenomenon, particularly impressive during spring floods when salmon leap upstream. The surrounding verdant riparian habitats and the mysterious Riezupe Sand Caves add to Kuldiga's natural attractions.

activities.text: Push on to the Riezupe sandstone caves for a striking contrast with yesterday's dunes: these tunnels carved in sandstone by natural erosion create a cool, mysterious underground labyrinth. Accessible on foot from the Venta riverbank. Flashlight useful, modest entrance fee.

Explore the atmospheric Riezupe Sand Caves, a network of tunnels carved into the sandstone banks near the Venta River. These caves, formed by centuries of natural erosion and human activity, create an otherworldly underground landscape unique to this region. The combination of waterfalls, caves, and historic architecture makes Kuldiga an unexpected highlight of any Latvian journey.

days_plan.itin_title: The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History

visits.text: The cobblestones of Riga's old town echo beneath Gothic, Baroque, and Neoclassical facades that succeed one another without logic—that's the beauty of the place, UNESCO-listed since 1997. The reconstructed House of the Blackheads presides over Town Hall Square; St. Peter's Church and its soaring spire organize the panorama. Allow half a day for the lanes and a pause in one of the square's many cafés.

More than 800 Art Nouveau facades in Riga's so-called 'Quiet Center'—the largest concentration in Europe. Alberta Street concentrates the most spectacular creations of Mikhail Eisenstein (father of the filmmaker): howling masks, winged women, and giant medallions on seven different buildings. Built between 1901 and 1908, no two are alike. Visit the Art Nouveau Museum at no. 12, closed Mondays.

Riga's Central Market is spectacular: five former zeppelin hangars converted into halls where Riga Black Balsam rubs shoulders with local cheeses and smoked meats. It's one of Europe's largest covered markets and the place where Riga has done its shopping since the 1930s. Arrive before 10 a.m. to see stalls at full capacity and prices before the tourist influx.

42 meters of granite, neither toppled nor moved under Soviet occupation: the Freedom Monument (Brīvības piemineklis) is one of the rare statues to have survived the Soviet era in place. Latvians affectionately call it 'Milda.' Erected in 1935, it represents a woman holding three golden stars symbolizing the country's historic regions. Permanent honor guard.

The Latvian Ethnographic Open-Air Museum gathers more than 100 historic buildings—farms, windmills, workshops—transferred from across the country into a forest park on Riga's edge. It's rural Latvian life across several centuries made visible in one walk. Seasonal events with traditional crafts and folk dances make the visit immersive.

Rigans call it 'the cathedral of music': Riga Dome Cathedral houses one of Europe's largest organs, built in 1884 and since restored. This 13th-century Romanesque-Gothic cathedral is the largest in the Baltic States and served as Lutheran church, Soviet warehouse, then concert hall. Organ concerts are held regularly—check the program before you go.

Explore Riga's UNESCO-listed Old Town, a labyrinthine medieval quarter considered one of the most handsome historic centers in Europe. Cobblestone streets wind beneath the soaring spire of St. Peter's Church while the magnificently reconstructed House of the Blackheads stands as a proud symbol of the city's resilience after Soviet domination. This atmospheric quarter reveals centuries of history through its Gothic, Baroque, Renaissance, and classical architecture.

Discover Riga's Art Nouveau District, home to over 800 stunning buildings featuring intricate facades, ornate sculptures, and extraordinary decorative details. Alberta iela (Alberta Street) showcases some of the finest examples of this turn-of-the-century European design anywhere on the continent. This UNESCO-recognized architectural treasure trove represents one of the world's most impressive concentrations of Art Nouveau buildings.

Wander through Riga Central Market, one of Europe's largest and most atmospheric food markets housed in five massive former Zeppelin hangars. This vibrant hub of local life offers an incredible array of fresh produce, meats, cheeses, and traditional Latvian specialties including grey peas with bacon and the famous Riga Black Balsam herbal liquor. The market provides an authentic window into daily Latvian life and culinary traditions.

Visit the Freedom Monument, an iconic 42-meter granite and copper monument that has stood as a symbol of Latvian independence and national identity since 1935. This beloved landmark, affectionately known as 'Milda,' depicts a woman holding three golden stars representing Latvia's historical regions. The monument survived Soviet occupation and remains the focal point of national celebrations and commemorations.

activities.text: If you arrive on a weekday, the market is less crowded and vendors more available to explain their products. Taste the local craft beer and grey peas with smoked bacon, a Latvian specialty. The halls generally close at 5 p.m.; best to spend the morning there and devote the afternoon to the Art Nouveau district.

Take the tram to Mežaparks park to leave the center and see the residential Riga of the 1920s-1930s: Art Nouveau villas and private gardens in a quarter the guides avoid. The park itself overlooks Lake Ķīšezers—good option for a picnic. Return by tram to the center in 20 minutes.

Explore the Latvian Ethnographic Open-Air Museum on Riga's outskirts, bringing centuries of Latvian rural life vividly to life. More than 100 historic buildings including traditional farmsteads, workshops, and windmills showcase the country's diverse regional heritage. Seasonal events featuring traditional craft workshops and folk dance performances make it an immersive cultural experience.

Explore the Latvian Ethnographic Open-Air Museum on the outskirts of Riga, bringing centuries of rural Latvian life to vivid reality. More than 100 historical buildings including traditional farmsteads, workshops, and windmills showcase the country's diverse regional heritage. Seasonal events featuring traditional craft workshops and folk dance performances make this an immersive cultural experience.

days_plan.itin_title: The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History

visits.text: Versailles on a smaller scale, Rastrelli more restrained: Rundāle Palace, designed by Francesco Bartolomeo Rastrelli (architect of St. Petersburg's Winter Palace), was built in two phases between 1736 and 1768 for Duke Ernst Johann Biron, favorite of Empress Anna Ivanovna. The 10-hectare French garden, with its rose garden and Green Theatre, won the European Garden Award in 2021. About 40 of the 138 rooms are open to the public.

days_plan.itin_title: The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days

visits.text: Built in 1214 on the orders of Bishop Albert of Riga, Turaida Castle dominates the Gauja valley like a red-brick vessel in the foliage. Restored in the 20th century after a 1776 fire, it's now one of Latvia's most-visited museums. The main tower offers a 360° view over the valley that local guides compare to the Latvian Switzerland.

The sandstone walls of Gutmanis Cave preserve inscriptions carved since the 17th century—hundreds of travelers have left their names in the soft rock. It's one of the largest caverns in the Baltic States. The legend of the 'Rose of Turaida,' a young girl who chose death over abduction, has been attached to this place since the 13th century.

Discover Sigulda, the gateway to Latvia's legendary Gauja Valley where misty woodlands, meandering rivers, and castle-topped hills have inspired countless local legends. The dramatic landscape of this region, often called the 'Switzerland of Latvia,' features undulating terrain, deep river valleys, and forests that glow with color in autumn. Cherry trees bloom throughout the charming town center, which also boasts elegant pre-independence architecture.

Explore the magnificent Turaida Castle, Latvia's answer to Transylvania's Bran Castle, whose keeps, bulwarks, and crenellations rise dramatically above the Gauja Valley. This 13th-century red-brick fortress has been beautifully restored and offers panoramic views across the forested landscape. The castle museum chronicles 800 years of regional history within its atmospheric towers and chambers.

Venture into the massive Gutmanis Cave, one of the largest caverns in the Baltic states, where ancient inscriptions dating back to the 17th century cover the sandstone walls. This natural wonder has been a place of legend and pilgrimage for centuries, with countless visitors leaving their marks on its surfaces. The cave's impressive scale and historical graffiti make it a unique attraction within the Gauja Valley.

activities.text: Hike in Gauja National Park, Latvia's largest and oldest, created in 1973. The trail linking Sigulda to Turaida (5.4 km one way) follows the river and Devonian sandstone escarpments. In autumn, the forest turns red and gold—the best season for this section. Bobsleigh and luge activities available in winter.

Descend into the secret Soviet bunkers of Līgatne, hidden deep in the forests on the edge of town. These underground facilities were designed to shelter high-ranking officials in case of nuclear war and offer a fascinating glimpse into Cold War-era Latvia. The well-preserved bunkers provide an eerie journey back to a time of superpower tensions and nuclear anxiety.

Experience outdoor adventures in Gauja National Park, Latvia's largest and most visited national park offering excellent hiking, canoeing, and Nordic skiing opportunities. The striking sandstone gorges and fir-forest-topped escarpments of Devonian rock create dramatic scenery throughout the park. The Ērgļu Cliffs and numerous nature trails invite exploration of this pristine Baltic wilderness.

days_plan.itin_title: The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History

visits.text: Founded in the second half of the 13th century, Ventspils Castle of the Livonian Order is one of Latvia's best-preserved medieval fortresses—it has kept its original footprint for 700 years. Fortress, residence, garrison, school, prison: the castle has done it all before becoming the Ventspils Museum in 2001, featuring an award-winning digital exhibition.

Flower beds at every intersection: Ventspils claims the title of Latvia's floral capital and earns it every summer. The city adds floral sculptures and parterres to its promenades from spring onwards. The market square and Skolas Street show the mix of civic pride and historic architecture that distinguishes this port city.

Visit Ventspils Castle, the best-preserved Livonian Order castle in Latvia, rising in pretty yellow-washed facades against the cobblestones of the Old Town. This 13th-century fortress has witnessed 800 years of Baltic history and now houses a museum chronicling the region's medieval heritage. The castle's strategic position reflects Ventspils' importance as one of the busiest ports in the Baltic region.

Stroll along Ventspils' famous flower-lined promenades that have earned the city its reputation as the flower capital of Latvia. From springtime onwards, blooming beds of flowers and bulbs erupt throughout the town and along its seaside walkways. The charming Market Square and historic homes on Skolas Street showcase the city's blend of history and civic pride.

activities.text: Stroll the Blue Flag-certified beach of Ventspils before heading to Jūrmala: the city has invested massively in its coastal amenities and the result is worth seeing. Harbor promenade, monumental 23-ton anchor, little 1916 steam train: Ventspils is also an unexpected playground. Take the tourist train to reach the beach from the center.

Enjoy Ventspils' well-maintained beaches and portside promenades where modern maritime industry meets traditional Baltic seaside charm. The city offers excellent facilities for families, including the popular Ventspils Children's Town with its interactive science center and playgrounds. The combination of medieval heritage, floral beauty, and coastal recreation makes Ventspils a surprisingly rewarding destination.

days_plan.itin_title: The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History

visits.text: Explore Daugavpils, Latvia's second city and birthplace of the renowned artist Mark Rothko, nestled in the hills and lakes of the scenic Daugava River valley. This town ticks to a Russian rhythm as the heartland of Latvia's Russian-speaking population, offering a fascinatingly different cultural experience from Riga. The glorious historical core features refined 19th-century architecture courtesy of St. Petersburg's influence on this borderland city.

Visit the impressive Daugavpils Fortress, whose looming bulwarks mark one of the militaristic hubs of the former Russian Empire. This 19th-century fortification, the only early 19th-century fortress in Northern Europe that has retained its original features, covers a vast area and once garrisoned thousands of troops. The fortress is currently being restored and offers a remarkable journey through Baltic military history.

Admire the Saints Boris and Gleb Cathedral, whose golden-hued onion domes and Orthodox cupolas rise dramatically against the Daugavpils skyline. This Russian Orthodox cathedral, with its architecture reminiscent of Kiev and Moscow's St. Basil's, reflects the strong Russian cultural influence in this region. The ornate interior with its traditional iconostasis creates an atmosphere of spiritual grandeur.

activities.text: Wander the streets of Daugavpils to discover the antithesis of bustling Riga in this contemplative provincial capital. The city's unique position between East and West has created a distinctive cultural atmosphere where Russian and Latvian traditions blend. Museums, galleries, and the Mark Rothko Art Centre honor the city's most famous son while showcasing contemporary Baltic art.

days_plan.itin_title: Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History

visits.text: Explore Slītere National Park, the natural jewel of the Kurzeme region encompassing the beautiful Blue Hills and great stretches of undeveloped Baltic coastline. Rolling sand dunes and sloping golden beaches meet the whitecaps of the sea in this protected wilderness area. Deep virgin forests of fir, elm, and rare Baltic ivy cover the interior, sheltering diverse wildlife in one of Latvia's most pristine environments.

Stand at Cape Kolka, the dramatic point where the park juts into the ocean and the waters of the Baltic Sea meet the Gulf of Riga. This remote headland offers spectacular views across the turbulent waters where two distinct bodies of water collide. The cape's isolated beauty and powerful natural forces create an atmosphere of wild, untamed nature.

activities.text: Discover the historic fishing villages scattered throughout Slītere National Park, many of which remained truly off-the-beaten-track during Soviet times due to a restricted military presence in the area. These rustic settlements preserve traditional Livonian fishing culture and architecture that has largely disappeared elsewhere. The combination of pristine nature and authentic heritage makes this region uniquely rewarding for adventurous travelers.

days_plan.itin_title: Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History

visits.text: Discover Liepaja, a fascinating patchwork of Art Nouveau architecture and Russian Orthodox heritage near the Lithuanian border. The Cathedral of the Holy Trinity dominates the cityscape with its soaring spire and houses one of the largest mechanical organs in the world. Bustling daily markets and blue-collar working neighborhoods give this 90,000-strong port city an authentic, lived-in character far from typical tourist routes.

Explore Karosta, a former clandestine Soviet military settlement on the edge of modern Liepaja that once housed over 25,000 military personnel. The windswept remnants of tsarist and USSR forts are now being transformed into art galleries and cultural spaces, while the haunting Karosta Prison offers visitors the chance to experience life as a Soviet-era prisoner. The dramatic northern beaches stretching along the coast add natural beauty to this evocative district.

activities.text: Tour the infamous Karosta Prison, one of Latvia's most unusual attractions where visitors can experience an immersive night as a prisoner in this former Soviet military detention facility. The stark, grim cells and military drills recreate the harsh conditions that actual prisoners endured. This unforgettable experience provides a visceral connection to Latvia's complex 20th-century history.

days_plan.itin_title: Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History

visits.text: Relax on the quieter beaches of Saulkrasti, Latvia's lesser-known summertime retreat facing Jurmala across the waters of the Riga Gulf. This more laid-back alternative to its famous counterpart offers miles of winding cycle tracks and gorgeous ivory sands at resort areas like Pabazi. The peaceful atmosphere attracts those seeking tranquility rather than the bustle of larger resorts.

Climb the White Dune, an 18-meter-high landmark rising dramatically on the edge of the Inčupe River. This natural formation offers prime sunset viewing across the Baltic coastline and surrounding forests of swaying linden and fir trees. The dune's elevated position provides panoramic perspectives across Saulkrasti's beautiful stretch of coastline.

activities.text: Hike the clifftop walking trails that wind through seaside forests along Saulkrasti's scenic coastline. These paths offer ever-changing views of the Baltic Sea through gaps in the dense woodland canopy. The combination of beach, forest, and coastal bluffs creates diverse landscapes within a compact area perfect for nature walks.

days_plan.itin_title: Latvia Road Trip: Medieval Castles, Baltic Coast and Soviet-Era History
